column1: 1. Overview of the Avatamsaka Sutra's Structure

The Avatamsaka Sutra exists in multiple translations, with the most complete being the 80-volume and 40-volume versions (translated by Śikṣānanda and Prajñā, respectively). The following tables outline the major sections and themes.
Table 1: Major Books/Chapters of the Avatamsaka Sutra

Includes teachings on interdependence, Buddha realms, and vows.

Below is a detailed breakdown of the Avatamsaka Sutra (Huayan Jing) by version, including all major books/chapters and their numbered lists (excluding Suddhodana’s teachers). The two primary versions are the 80-volume (translated by Śikṣānanda, Tang Dynasty) and the 40-volume (translated by Prajñā, late Tang). I’ve also included the 60-volume version (Buddhabhadra, Eastern Jin) for reference.
Table 1: Avatamsaka Sutra Versions and Their Books/Chapters

Interpenetration: A core theme—each book reflects the whole (e.g., one dust mote contains the entire cosmos).
Commentaries: The Huayan school expanded these lists into systematic treatises (e.g., Fazang’s Treatise on the Five Teachings).
Variations: The 40-volume version merges some books for conciseness.

are there other core teachings that are part of a series that should be included from the text?

Yes! The Avatamsaka Sutra contains several other core teachings organized in numbered series beyond the Ten Stages, Ten Treasuries, and Ten Dedications. Below are the key lists and their doctrinal significance, drawn from across the sutra’s versions:
Table 1: Additional Numbered Teachings in the Avatamsaka Sutra

column4: 1. Phenomenal (事), 2. Principle (理), 3. Non-obstruction (事理無礙), 4. Interpenetration (事事無礙).

1. Universality, 2. Particularity, 3. Unity, 4. Diversity, 5. Formation, 6. Emptiness.
Gates to understand interpenetration (e.g., "All in One, One in All").

column3: All phenomena arise together in mutual dependence.

column2: Using the World to Explain Dharma
column3: Conventional reality points to ultimate truth.
Wholeness (e.g., a house as a single entity).
Distinct parts (e.g., the house’s roof, walls).
Harmony of parts forming a whole.

column1: Interpenetration: The core theme—every part of the sutra reflects the whole (e.g., one dust mote contains all Buddha-realms).
Cosmology: The Avatamsaka describes infinite Buddha-fields (e.g., the "Lotus World" of Vairocana).
Sudhana’s Journey: Each of his 53 teachers embodies one aspect of the Dharma, collectively illustrating the path.
